sections.heading: Complications
sections.content: {"disease_related": [{"complication": "Bone metastases and skeletal-related events", "management": "Denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks (category 1, preferred) or zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks for prevention of symptomatic SREs in patients with bone-metastatic CRPC. EBRT for palliation of painful bone metastases. Radium-223 for symptomatic bone metastases without visceral metastases. Bisphosphonates or denosumab for bone loss prevention in castration-sensitive patients on ADT based on FRAX risk assessment."}, {"complication": "Spinal cord compression", "management": "Emergency MRI for suspected spinal cord compression; emergent decompressive surgery if feasible followed by RT, or RT alone. EBRT can be considered for patients with limited life expectancy."}, {"complication": "Pathologic fracture", "management": "Surgical fixation as needed; antiresorptive therapy with denosumab or zoledronic acid for prevention; calcium and vitamin D supplementation; evaluation of fall risk; weight-bearing exercises."}, {"complication": "Urinary obstruction", "management": "TURP for bladder outlet obstruction; nephrostomy tubes or ureteral stents for ureteral obstruction; palliative RT to prostate if appropriate."}, {"complication": "Visceral metastases (liver, lung, adrenal, brain)", "management": "Systemic therapy based on prior treatments and biomarker status; palliative RT for symptom control; chemotherapy with platinum-based regimens if neuroendocrine features present."}, {"complication": "Local recurrence after definitive therapy", "management": "PSA monitoring per post-treatment protocols; secondary therapy options include RT (for post-RP recurrence), local secondary therapy including cryotherapy, HIFU, IRE, reirradiation, or RP (for post-RT recurrence); systemic therapy if metastatic disease present."}, {"complication": "Progression to neuroendocrine/small cell prostate cancer", "management": "Biopsy of metastatic lesions to identify small cell/neuroendocrine histology; cytotoxic chemotherapy with cisplatin/etoposide, carboplatin/etoposide, docetaxel/carboplatin, or cabazitaxel/carboplatin; consult NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer for additional options."}], "treatment_related": {"chemotherapy": ["Docetaxel: neutropenia, leukopenia, febrile neutropenia, neutropenic infections, fluid retention, hypersensitivity reaction, hepatic function impairment, neuropathy, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, alopecia, diarrhea", "Cabazitaxel: febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), nausea/vomiting, anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%), sepsis, renal failure; lower neuropathy than docetaxel especially at 20 mg/m2", "Mitoxantrone: similar adverse events to docetaxel but lower rates of grade 3-4 neutropenic fevers, cardiovascular events, nausea and vomiting, metabolic disturbances, and neurologic events"], "immunotherapy": ["Sipuleucel-T: mild to moderate chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%); usually transient; not recommended if visceral metastases present", "Pembrolizumab: severe immune-mediated adverse reactions including pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, myocarditis, endocrinopathies, exfoliative dermatologic conditions, renal failure, nephritis, ocular toxicities"], "targeted_therapy": ["PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib): anemia (including requiring transfusion), fatigue, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, weight loss, diarrhea, thrombocytopenia, creatinine elevation; rare but serious: thromboembolic events, drug-induced pneumonitis, theoretical risk of myelodysplasia or acute myeloid leukemia", "PARP inhibitor combinations with ARPIs: higher rates of anemia (29% with niraparib/abiraterone vs 4.6% control), hypertension, and grade 3-4 adverse events (75% vs 59% for niraparib arm)", "Abiraterone: hypertension, hypokalemia, edema, fatigue, hot flushes, liver toxicity, cardiac events (rare atrial fibrillation)", "Enzalutamide: fatigue, seizures (0.9%), hypertension", "Apalutamide: rash (24%), fracture (11%), hypothyroidism (8%)", "Darolutamide: fatigue, pain in extremity, rash", "Lu-177-PSMA-617: anemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, fatigue"], "surgery": ["Radical prostatectomy: urinary incontinence (20-50% requiring ≥1 pad/day), erectile dysfunction (40-60%), penile shortening, urethral or bladder neck contracture/stricture; anastomotic strictures increase risk of long-term incontinence", "Pelvic lymph node dissection: lymphocele, lymphedema, deep vein thrombosis, vascular injury", "Secondary RP after RT: significantly higher morbidity including incontinence, erectile dysfunction, bladder neck contracture, and rectal injury"], "radiation": ["External beam RT: acute GI toxicity (rectal urgency, frequency, diarrhea), acute GU toxicity (urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria); late GI toxicity (rectal bleeding, fistula); late GU toxicity (urethral stricture, hematuria)", "Brachytherapy: urinary frequency, urgency, retention; erectile dysfunction; rectal injury; urethral stricture"]}}

cards.name: Cabazitaxel - severe diarrhea
cards.detail: Cabazitaxel: febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), nausea/vomiting, anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%), sepsis, renal failure; lower neuropathy than docetaxel especially at 20 mg/m2

cards.name: Cabazitaxel - fatigue
cards.detail: Cabazitaxel: febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), nausea/vomiting, anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%), sepsis, renal failure; lower neuropathy than docetaxel especially at 20 mg/m2

cards.name: Cabazitaxel - anemia
cards.detail: Cabazitaxel: febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), nausea/vomiting, anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%), sepsis, renal failure; lower neuropathy than docetaxel especially at 20 mg/m2

cards.name: Cabazitaxel - thrombocytopenia
cards.detail: Cabazitaxel: febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), nausea/vomiting, anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%), sepsis, renal failure; lower neuropathy than docetaxel especially at 20 mg/m2

cards.name: Sipuleucel-T - chills
cards.detail: Sipuleucel-T: mild to moderate chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%); usually transient; not recommended if visceral metastases present

cards.name: Sipuleucel-T - pyrexia
cards.detail: Sipuleucel-T: mild to moderate chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%); usually transient; not recommended if visceral metastases present

cards.name: Sipuleucel-T - headache
cards.detail: Sipuleucel-T: mild to moderate chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%); usually transient; not recommended if visceral metastases present

cards.name: Radical prostatectomy - urinary incontinence
cards.detail: Radical prostatectomy: urinary incontinence (20-50% requiring ≥1 pad/day), erectile dysfunction (40-60%), penile shortening, urethral or bladder neck contracture/stricture; anastomotic strictures increase risk of long-term incontinence

cards.name: Radical prostatectomy - erectile dysfunction
cards.detail: Radical prostatectomy: urinary incontinence (20-50% requiring ≥1 pad/day), erectile dysfunction (40-60%), penile shortening, urethral or bladder neck contracture/stricture; anastomotic strictures increase risk of long-term incontinence

cards.name: PARP combo - anemia
cards.detail: PARP inhibitor combinations with ARPIs: higher rates of anemia (29% with niraparib/abiraterone vs 4.6% control), hypertension, and grade 3-4 adverse events (75% vs 59% for niraparib arm)

cards.name: PARP combo - grade 3-4 adverse events
cards.detail: PARP inhibitor combinations with ARPIs: higher rates of anemia (29% with niraparib/abiraterone vs 4.6% control), hypertension, and grade 3-4 adverse events (75% vs 59% for niraparib arm)
